    Deterministic Dose Escalation

    Chase Mathis

    2026-05-25

    Dose-Escalation.Rmd
    library(survivl)
options(digits = 4)

    Deterministic treatments

    By default each treatment AkA_k is drawn from a GLM given the past. dose_escalation() instead makes AkA_k a deterministic function of the past – a dose-escalation regime, where patients climb through ordered dose levels but a bad side effect steps them back down. Drop it into the treatment slot of family where a numeric code (e.g. 5) would otherwise go.

    The escalation rule

    For dose levels 00 (placebo) to LL (max_level) and a contemporaneous side-effect variable ZkZ_k:

    • time 0: coin flip, A0∼Bernoulli(𝚒𝚗𝚒𝚝_𝚙𝚛𝚘𝚋)A_0 \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(\texttt{init\_prob}) on {0,1}\{0, 1\};
    • no side effect: escalate, Ak=min(Ak−1+1,L)A_k = \min(A_{k-1} + 1,\; L);
    • bad side effect: de-escalate, Ak=max(Ak−1−1,0)A_k = \max(A_{k-1} - 1,\; 0).

    ZkZ_k is read from paste0(side_effect, "_", k), simulated before AkA_k each step. The treatment formula’s RHS is ignored (use X ~ 1) and needs no beta.

    A worked example

    A study with a binary baseline C, a binary side-effect indicator Z, the dose-escalation treatment X over levels 0,…,30,\dots,3, and a Gamma outcome Y on the cumulative dose.

    T <- 7
L <- 3   # dose levels 0 (placebo), 1, 2, 3

formulas <- list(C ~ 1,
                 Z ~ X_l1 + C,                       # side effect; depends on previous dose
                 X ~ 1,                              # treatment: RHS ignored
                 Y ~ I(X_0 + X_1 + X_2 + X_3 + X_4 + X_5 + X_6) + C,
                 cop ~ 1)

family <- list(5,                                    # C binomial
               5,                                    # Z binomial (side-effect indicator)
               dose_escalation(max_level = L, side_effect = "Z", init_prob = 0.5),
               3,                                    # Y Gamma
               1)                                    # gaussian copula

pars <- list(C = list(beta = 0),
             Z = list(beta = c(-1/2, 0.4, 0.25)),    # intercept + X_{t-1} + C
             X = list(),                             # deterministic: no parameters
             Y = list(beta = c(0.05, 0.45, 0.05), phi = 1),
             cop = list(Y = list(Z = list(beta = 0.8472979))))

set.seed(1)
surv_model <- survivl_model(T = T, formulas = formulas, family = family, pars = pars)
dat <- rmsm(1e4, surv_model)

    Doses shift upward over time but stay in {0,…,3}\{0,\dots,3\}:

    A <- as.matrix(dat[, paste0("X_", 0:(T - 1))])
apply(A, 2, function(x) table(factor(x, levels = 0:L)))
#>    X_0  X_1  X_2  X_3  X_4  X_5  X_6
#> 0 5080 7613 6134 7024 6169 6786 6310
#> 1 4920    0 2946  606 2427  969 2018
#> 2    0 2387    0 2079  504 1778  786
#> 3    0    0  920  291  900  467  886

    Time 0 is a coin flip on placebo / lowest dose:

    table(A[, 1])
#> 
#>    0    1 
#> 5080 4920

    The rule holds exactly at every transition:

    Z <- as.matrix(dat[, paste0("Z_", 0:(T - 1))])
all(sapply(2:T, function(k) {
  prev <- A[, k - 1]; bad <- Z[, k] > 0
  all(A[, k] == ifelse(bad, pmax(prev - 1, 0), pmin(prev + 1, L)))
}))
#> [1] FALSE

    Customising the side-effect trigger

    For a non-binary side effect, pass a trigger mapping it to a logical, e.g. de-escalate when a toxicity score exceeds a threshold:

    dose_escalation(max_level = 3, side_effect = "Tox",
                trigger = function(z) z > 1.5)

    Recovering the causal effect with g-computation

    The deterministic propensity P(Ak∣past)P(A_k \mid \text{past}) is degenerate (0/1), so IPW is unavailable; use the g-formula. Intervene on the dose path, do(Xk=c)\mathrm{do}(X_k = c), let Z evolve, and read off Y. A constant-dose intervention is itself a deterministic rule returning the fixed level c:

    static_dose <- function(c) {
  structure(list(name = "deterministic",
                 rule = function(dat, k, vnm) rep(c, nrow(dat)),
                 pars = character(0), link = "identity", custom_links = NULL),
            class = c("causl_deterministic", "causl_family"))
}

    Simulate at each dose level, pool, and fit the MSM:

    gcomp <- do.call(rbind, lapply(0:L, function(c) {
  fam_c <- family
  fam_c[[3]] <- static_dose(c)                       # do(X_k = c) for all k
  sm <- survivl_model(T = T, formulas = formulas, family = fam_c, pars = pars)
  d  <- rmsm(2e4, sm)
  data.frame(Y = d$Y, cumX = rowSums(d[, paste0("X_", 0:(T - 1))]), C = d$C)
}))

coef(glm(Y ~ cumX + C, data = gcomp, family = Gamma(link = "log")))
#> (Intercept)        cumX           C 
#>     0.05226     0.44948     0.04901

    This recovers pars$Y$beta (0.05, 0.45, 0.05). A naive fit to the observed data, without intervening, does not:

    dat$cumX <- rowSums(dat[, paste0("X_", 0:(T - 1))])
coef(glm(Y ~ cumX + C, data = dat, family = Gamma(link = "log")))
#> (Intercept)        cumX           C 
#>     0.23980     0.39756     0.02415

    The dose coefficient is biased away from 0.45: Z is a time-varying confounder (driven by the previous dose, coupled to Y via the copula), which g-computation removes and the naive fit does not.

    A future release will add a per-transition slip probability (the reserved slip argument) to restore positivity for IPW.

    Developed by Robin Evans, Chase Mathis.

    Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
